text: Working space for the book on the child welfare system and the eventual cohort workbook. Write a little most mornings before the kids are up.
Quick links
Open the Chapters database
Working arc: open with one family's story, then widen to how the system around them is built — and where it fails the people inside it.
Parts
Part I: The Knock — Part II: The Caseload — Part III: The People Who Stay
Decide whether the workbook is its own thing or an appendix
Why I stayed seven years when so many leave in two: it isn't the paperwork, it's the families you can't stop thinking about.
Beats to hit
First home visit, the mentor who taught me to listen, the night I almost quit
Tighten the opening scene — too much backstory up front
Composite notes from conversations with foster parents and former caseworkers. Names and identifying details removed — composites only, never a real case.
Themes
What surprised them most; what they wish they'd been told before licensing
Get consent language reviewed before quoting anyone
Module 1: Are you ready? A self-check for prospective foster parents — drawn from what the PRIDE cohort actually wrestles with in week one.
Sections
Reflection prompts, a household-readiness checklist, and a 'questions to ask your agency' page
Test the reflection prompts with the fall 2026 cohort
Workbook spans the same ten weeks as the cohort — one module per couple of sessions, so it can be used alongside the class.
Module list
Readiness, attachment & loss, discipline that heals, working with birth families, the licensing maze
Map each module to its PRIDE session number
Title candidates — leaning toward something plain and human rather than clever.
Shortlist
'The People Who Stay' / 'Mandated' / 'The Knock at the Door'
Run the shortlist past the Page Turners book club
Background reading on how the modern foster-care system was built — for the Part II chapters that step back from the personal story.
Topics to cover
ASFA and its aftermath, kinship care, the federal funding maze
Cite sources properly — don't paraphrase from memory
Memoirs and reporting that do this kind of writing well — read for craft, not just content.
On the shelf
Random Family, Evicted, To the End of June
Finish To the End of June before the fall cohort starts
CPS home visit ran into the evening; safety assessment done.
PRIDE foster cohort starts Oct 6 — adapting the curriculum with my case studies.
Court testimony at Juvenile Court this morning, then back to the caseload.
Sunday pot roast from Dad's recipe; mafe next weekend from Uncle Oscar's playbook.
Choir rehearsal Thursday — alto, half therapy and half worship.
Nightly journal, one Moleskine a year, above the desk I rarely reread.
Wednesday dinner with Mom, six to eight-thirty, standing.
Therapy with Dr. Grant — court and removals are the triggers.
Raised beds out back, okra because Dad planted it, Fiona narrating the herbs.
Book club Saturday — I'm the one who finished the book again.
Marcus's Dunbar team looked sharp; David running the soccer carpool.
Andrew's teacher flagged attention concerns; monitoring, no eval yet.
Fiona's thyroid panel repeat on the 22nd, watching it.
Denise cooked tonight and ran the kitchen, spoiling the grandkids.
Caseload at 20 families; recommended max is 15, the constant overload.
Reset playlist for the hard nights — gospel and two Tom Petty songs.
Foster cohort roster in Sheets; 14 graduated last cohort.
Back PT twice a week; delaying the microdiscectomy for the caseload.
Uncle Oscar's Main Street Kitchen for the monthly family dinner.
Buspirone steady, Calm app three or four nights a week.
Secondary-trauma debrief with Brenda; the bureaucracy constrains her.
Senegal folder in email — almost-planning Dakar with the kids for ten years.
Lexington Market crab cakes, El Salvadoreno pupusas in Fells Point.
Front-stoop coffee chair in warm weather, the morning quiet.
